The main problem with Fire and Ash is that it doesn’t feel like the story is moving forward or offering anything new. Instead, it feels like a direct continuation of The Way of Water arc, essentially completing that storyline with the same “the family is our fortress” theme.
Rather than calling it Fire and Ash, it should have been titled The Way of Water: Vol. 2. The name Fire and Ash led people to expect a new chapter and something fresh in this world, which ultimately created expectations the film didn’t meet.

Just watched the film. I’m glad I didn’t watch this trailer. Shows waaayyyy too much. But then again, most movies do nowadays instead of just sticking to one trailer. Overall I enjoyed the movie. Absolutely gorgeous, and the pacing was quite good considering it’s over 3 hours. Still feels long, but not a slog nor does it feel like a chore to get through. Yes there were a lot of the same story beats from the first movie, but a lot of it was pulled off in a way that felt it was justified. I enjoyed Varang and think she was a good villain and the Ash People as a whole a good addition to the franchise.

Just watched Avatar 3 and it was truly stunning. ✨
A beautiful film, fully living up to the high expectations set by its visionary director, James Cameron.

It absolutely earns a 10/10 and stands proudly alongside its predecessor, without losing its soul, depth, or magic. The world-building, visuals, and emotional weight pull you in from the very first moment and never let go.

Once again, Cameron proves why he’s a master of cinema: vision, detail, and an immersive experience that you don’t just watch — you feel.
If you love films that transport you to another world, Avatar 3 is not just a sequel, it’s a promise fulfilled. 🎬🌊

12/18/2025 - Avatar Fire and Ash releases tomorrow. It came out early today and I would love to see it right now but I’m waiting for the big day. I have been waiting for this day for so long. I remember counting the days, liking those posts on instagram that would countdown (day678). And now we're here. It releases tomorrow and I've watched the trailers many many times. I’m so eager and excited and I know it will be a masterpiece. No one else but James Cameron can do something like this, no one would do it better. So much has changed and happened since the second movie
Some things that have happened since the second - 
- Went to Michigan, Missouri, Toronto, Chicago, Indiana, Iowa, Nebraska, Colorado Denver, and Utah 🌁
- Went to Arizona again
- Got a gf, it's interesting right now and spicy. Lot of juicy lore hehe
- In college studying health sciences
- Just finished my second semester, got an A in anatomy and physiology 151
- Figured out I wanna be a Physician Assistant
- Became an emt 🚑
- Have 2000 PCE
- Started selling my own on Depop
- Got some money in my bank account
- Finally bought some of the shoes ive been wanting since high school (Vaeline ones, Winterized Loyals, Yeezy Waverunners, Union) 
- Learned to drive, ambulances even 
- Have many work stories, including a patient yelling in downtown san diego and police come to escort us
- Played more TLOU 2 and got really good at grounded (even played survival Perm and lasted 7 hours) 🧟‍♂
- Met patients that have impacted me (the dts who was a nurse and wanted to speak with me)
- Changed my hairstyle 
- Lost 50 lbs
- My music taste went from rap to rap, spanish, regatton, pop, indie, metal, blues, alternative rock, and some country
- Explored a lot of San diego and Riverside county in california
- Learned that I love science , particularly biology - courses like microbiology, a and p, genetics, virology, also courses in chemistry 🧬🔬
- Learned a lot about myself
- Went to seattle twice 🏙
-Took a class in statistics and calculus at the same time in highschool, something i always dreamt of doing 
So much has changed for me since the release of Avatar The Way of Water. I hope the fourth film gets to see the light of day and James Cameron directs it. I hope hate doesn't kill the movie from ever coming out. And if we get to see Avatar 4, I am so eager to see how much will change for me. I still remmber writing my comment like this one for Avatar The Way of Water, and I remember not writing as much as right now. So much has changed since then. And I know so much will happen by the time the fourth releases. I am all here for it. For now, we celebrate this film. We appreciate the hard work the team did. And we enjoy this film we're going to witness all together. AVATAR FIRE AND ASH HERE WE COME!!
P.S. Im emotionally nervous as I might cry watching this movie, I pray Jake Sully doesn't die

I watched Avatar 3 🔥 (Spoiler warning ⚠️)

I’m disappointed… I don’t like that they weren’t able to come up with a new villain in the third installment. I mean, they added Varang and her tribe, but it would have been better if they hadn’t brought humans back in this part, and if they had finally let Quaritch die for good. It would have been much more interesting if this installment focused only on fighting another tribe, because the whole “humans” storyline has already been seen and already sold. The first time, I was thrilled, but this time I was just disappointed because they weren’t able to come up with something new. It would have been amazing to watch a story without humans—two tribes fighting each other and using their territories and skills in that battle.

I don’t like what they did with the connection to the Tree of Souls, making it look like you’re putting on virtual reality goggles. (I mean, wtf was that?) It was always shown that when connecting to the Tree of Souls, an avatar could only hear their voices (singing and laughing), not see them or talk to them. I also don’t like that they showed the face of Eywa—it was better when they left it to us to imagine her in our own way. (This version of Eywa looked like a poorly edited AI video.) I also don’t like that they made Kiri special, only for her to end up needing the help of a human and a 9-year-old child to reach Eywa. (So how is she special then?) And I don’t like her flying braid that she used to attack Varang—yes, it looked powerful, but not for this movie.

Overall, I sat there for three hours and watched something I’ve already seen before. They showed a lot, but in the end, they showed nothing.

This is a comment from a longtime fan who has loved Avatar since the very beginning. 💙

I’ve finally seen the latest installment of Avatar. I haven’t watched the critical video yet, but I wanted to share my personal impressions. Having seen the entire saga at least once, I still wonder whether I’ll ever rewatch the third film. What is certain is that revisiting The Abyss by James Cameron gave me a familiar feeling: it feels like all the themes he has already explored are resurfacing here. Even though I know he wrote the entire saga and shot this latest film at the same time as the second one, one might wonder whether the second film could simply have been split into two parts rather than giving birth to a new installment.

From a technical standpoint, the film is undeniably impressive. It surpasses most productions of the year in terms of visual achievement and stands as a true technological feat. Paradoxically, however, what had amazed me in the second film—the aquatic environment—did not impress me as much this time. The novelty effect has worn off, and the story felt secondary compared to the visual experience.

That said, I did enjoy certain aspects, particularly the almost childlike pleasure of recognizing Sigourney Weaver’s voice portraying a younger version of her character. It reminded me that cinema is also a game of memory and references, a dialogue between past and present. Yet this time, the film felt more theatrical, as if each scene were a stage act rather than part of a fluid cinematic narrative. And when you think about it, the Avatar saga itself fits squarely within the tradition of American westerns: the clash of cultures, the fight for territory, and the exploration of society’s margins. It is fascinating to see how these stories, though rooted in a brief and often misunderstood historical period, continue to resonate in a universal context.

On a philosophical level, what Cameron proposes remains relevant: the defense of the environment, the confrontation between humanity and nature, and the way intercultural relationships are portrayed. This timeless message transcends eras and locations. However, I must admit that I did not grow attached to the characters. The fact that the human hero is paraplegic is something I had completely forgotten, and the integration of elements from the second film into this one makes the narrative feel overly long and, at times, disjointed.

Overall, it’s a film that can be appreciated for its spectacle and its message, but one that leaves a strong sense of déjà vu. Cameron once again proves that he can create fascinating worlds, but he also reminds us that technical grandeur alone is not always enough to renew emotion. If this third installment truly closes the trilogy, it mainly invites reflection on the repetition of themes and on our relationship with time, memory, and history.

To put it more simply, I really disliked the way humans are portrayed in the film, particularly Spider and Colonel Miles Quaritch. The way they speak sounds completely artificial, and this undermines the credibility of the inhabitants of Pandora as a whole. For example, when the teenagers talk among themselves, it feels like watching a B-movie. That’s when I realize that, despite the visual beauty and the fascinating sci-fi elements of Avatar, the screenplay and the characters’ narrative arcs remain very conventional and ultimately quite unoriginal.

EDIT: I watched the video, and after thinking it over, I still feel conflicted. You can say what you want, but there are no major spoilers, and in the end, not much actually happens. We’re mostly being asked to wait for Avatar 4, and that’s precisely what frustrates me.

In the second film, I was genuinely happy: they were in the water, exploring the planet, and there was a real sense of discovery. Here, we always talk about James Cameron, but we shouldn’t forget that he’s not alone—he has an incredible team behind him.

That said, I truly hope they release the films more quickly, because at some point, he’s going to age, and even 2030 still feels very far away. What can be criticized is that he created an extraordinary alien species and an extraordinary planet, yet doesn’t exploit them as much as he could, in my opinion. I sometimes feel like he’s remaking the same film over and over, with the same stakes, as is often the case throughout his filmography.

So yes, it’s still one of the biggest films of the year, but it may not be the one that moved me the most on a narrative level, especially for a Christmas release. I’m a generous audience member, and I admit I might be a bit demanding because I watch a lot of films throughout the year.

People will clearly turn out in massive numbers, but I also hear many saying that it feels repetitive. Some even claim it’s better than the second one. However, from a technological standpoint, nothing comes close to Avatar—that much is undeniable.

My Theories In Which Seen Takes Place First And Last.




SPOLIERS!










































































































The Beginning Of The Film Will Deal With The Aftermath Of The Battle At The Three Brothers Rocks And The Sully Family Struggling With The Loss Of Neteyam.

During The Middle Of The First Act When The Sully's Meet The Wind Traders, They Are Attacked By Varang And Her Clan And Neytiri Gets Badly Injured During The Battle.

After That Happens Lo'ak Decides To Leave Jake Sully And Neytiri And Goes Out With The Kids Including Spider.

During The Middle Of The Film, Quaritch Along With Lyle Wainfleet Meanwhile Heads To The Mangkwan Clan And He Is Able To Form An Alliance With Varang.

They Head To The Metkayina Village Where They Proceed To Burn Down The Village And Jake Allows Himself To Be Captured.

Meanwhille With The Children, When The Kids Are Being Chased By The Mangkwan Clan And During The Chase At Night Spider's Mask Loses Power And He Faints.

Jake Is Brought To Bridgehead City Where He Is Forced To Tell Quaritch Where The Location Of His Children Are.

When Spider Wakes Up He Realizes That He Can Now Breathe Pandora's Air.

The Kids Are Then Caught By Varang And Her Tribe But Are Then Rescued By Jake Along With Quaritch Along With Wainfleet (In The New Clip). I Have No Idea Why.

Jake And His Family Prepare For Battle As Quaritch Brings A Massive Army To The Metkayina Area Along With Varang.

A Huge Battle Breaks Out And The Film Might End On A Big Cliffhanger.

The Opening Shot From The First Trailer Is Most Likely A Flashback Scene Where Lo'ak And Neteyam Are Flying On Their Ikran's Will Probably Happen During The First Half Of The Movie. 

Also The Still Image Of Quaritch Talking With Spider Will Probably Take Place Right After Jake And Quaritch Free The Children But Quaritch Takes Spider To Bridgehead.

And Those Are My Predictions

I'm definitely looking forward to this movie. While the first part was praised not so much for its graphics as for the philosophical questions it raised, and while the second film was somewhat disappointing, I'm eager to see how the conflict between humans and the Navies will unfold. Given the historical experience, technological superiority, and human factor, the Navies are doomed. However, it's unlikely that the censors will allow a genocide of the locals, so it's interesting to see how the screenwriter will navigate this without making it seem like a giveaway from the humans. With such technology and a lack of moral guidance, it's easy to solve the Pandoran issue by force, even with Eywa's intervention.

When the first Avatar came out, even though I have seen it like 3 times in the cinemas, I did not care for its story or the world. I was just there for the 3D. Then, I really enjoyed the second film a lot more, even though the story seemed repeat of number 1 in many ways. Obviously, the visuals mesmerised me. Now, the 3rd film still seems to be revolving around the same conflict, I know I won't care too much about the story. But there is no other film than Avatar, in the past 15 years, that looks this visually spectacular. I have always loved Cameron as a director. Terminator 1 and 2 some of my most favourite films. I would love him to be doing more stuff like that. But he is such an amazing world builder, there is no way not to be immersed in the worlds he creates. And Pandora is the most intricate one he has ever created. It is so dense with details, it feels like a real place. There is nothing like this in cinema anymore. I am so glad he is carrying the torch that Lucas lit up with the Prequel trilogy of trying to create really immersive, intricate alien worlds to transport people to far far places with the magic of cinema!

Sonnenfeld felt that the Addamses would take Gordon in regardless of whether he was the real Fester or not, while the actors felt that the audience would find it awfully unsatisfying to tease Fester the whole movie and then confirm he was a fake. They asked the ending be changed, and Sonnenfeld saw their reasoning, so he acquiesced. (Electing Christina Ricci to speak on their behalf certainly can't have hurt their case)

To basically recap what Avatar has:
- Native people fighting an invading force
- Natives Intermingling with people (humans) who came and had good intentions
- A US-backed organization inciting wars and providing arms to corporate interest allies for a resource underground
- Said organization's fighting force is headed by a guy that believes in might is right
- Gets rid of scientists when they aren't useful for exploiting the planet
- Uses advanced technology to wage war on said natives 

You'd have to be asleep to not understand the story that is being told. Yet somehow I feel like the point is lost on most people in my country.

Meine Theorie und Idee zur AVATAR-Gesamtstory nach Teil 2 war:
Nach dem Unobtanium-Schwebe-Gestein aus Teil 1 ist die RDA von der Erde nun hinter dem lebensverlängernden Hirndrüsen-Sekret der Wale auf Pandora her. Und die Sully-Tochter hat eine quasi magische Verbindung mit der dortigen Natur bzw. Eywa, also eine Art Superkraft und "die Macht". Und die Invasion von der Erde durch die RDA hörte nach Teil 1 nicht nur nicht auf, sondern die Raubbau-Firmen-Soldaten kamen von dort noch aggressiver und stärker(ausgerüstet) zurück.

Daher war meine Idee, dass alles von Jake Sully und Co. nur so beendet werden und Frieden einkehren kann, indem ein Teil von ihnen mit den RDA-Raumschiffen zur Erde fliegt - und zwar mit allen Pflanzen von Pandora in Form von Samen (und The Seed Bearer soll ja angeblich mal ein Titel für einen Teil gewesen sein laut Internet). Um diese Flora auf der kaputten Erde zu pflanzen, so die Menschheit zu retten und das gewaltsame kriegerisch-mörderische Einfallen der RDA auf Pandora somit für immer obsolet zu machen und zu beenden. Und die Pandora-Pflanzen könnten erst nicht auf der Erde durch die dort ganz anderen Bedingungen wachsen. Was dann die Tochter von Jake Sully mit ihre Kraft/Macht/Gabe doch hinkriegen könnte/würde. Und dass jetzt schon der Junge namens Tarzan als Mensch ohne Maske auf Pandora atmen und (über)leben kann, bestätigt mich darin nun sogar noch mehr.

Und danach könnte es sogar noch hyper-epischer werden, indem Jake und Co. es mit dem Beleben/Bepflanzen bzw. Terra... äh, Pandoraforming nicht bei der so geretteten Erde und  Menschheit belassen, sondern mit dem lebensverlängernden Wal-Sekret noch viel viel weiter von Pandora bzw. der Erde weg andere Welten retten oder eben überhaupt mit Leben aktivieren könnten. Sie würden also eventuell teils Jahrhundert weit und lang in andere ferne Sternensysteme oder gar Galaxien reisen, auch Avatare/Na'vi und Erden-Menschen schon vereint, am Leben gehalten durch das Wal-Sekret und so das Leben, die Pandora-Pflanzen und auch eventuelle sich(/einen Teil der jeweiligen Raumschiff-Besatzungen im ganzen Kosmos verteilen. Auch hier hat James Cameron in einem Interview mit Denis Villeneuve gesagt, dass es in Teil 4 einen größeren Zeitsprung von 10 oder mehr Jahren geben wird, weshalb er die vorherigen Szenen mit den Kinder-Darstellern davor schon längst gedreht hat, ehe die da rauswachsen bzw. neue Darsteller die älteren Sully-Kinder spielen werden - was ebenfalls Hoffnung auf diese mögliche geile Lösung und Story-Wendung macht.

Das wäre zumindest meine Ideal-Vorstellung zu einem mehr als wuchtig-würdigen Avatar-Finale und würde alles mehr als rechtfertigen an  kosmisch-epischer Geschichte (und Wartezeit auf die jeweiligen Kino-Filme). Also bleibe ich mal gespannt, ob es so gut und sinnig wird oder am Ende ganz anders. Aber da es Master James Cameron ist, vertraue ich einfach mal auf eine runde und intelligent abschließende Sache mit Teil 5, die all die Jahrzehnte seines Lebens für die dann fünf Teile an Arbeit wert sein wird bzw. sollte. Der gute Mann hat schließlich noch nie enttäuscht oder etwas nur Halbgares abgeliefert.

I got no doubt i truly like this movie but i gonna beg to differ here at risk of being wrong but will i truly love it? hats off to Cameron he has truly proven to be king of the box office across multiple decades no question about it and the ability of 'Way of Water' to break Avatar 1's record is phenomenal however... in a number of ways it not hard to see why the oscars (i point out more people not less have deep reservation on their judgement of who wins and their tastes of who contends for big film prizes in most of last decade) but that aside The Way of Water had some notable pacing problems that the first Avatar did not have.. it run time was too much and cinematically it box office record breaking status aside.. i just felt it lacked the fluency and consistent punch across it runtime to the original 'Avatar' visually each Avatar movie gets to a whole new level no doubting that BUT cinematically is Cameron becoming victim to his own original AVATAR'S success? in terms of both lesser critical acclaim than the first film and less prominence in the oscars... but also as a viewing experience in terms of fluency of the narrative was 'way of water' as brilliant as the first one at such a high level? it was not... in thsi sense the fire and ash avatar 3 trailer is very impressive but did it blow me away? no not till i see it can i determine that in cinemas and i WILL absolutely... but seemed to me bulk of the trailer in 'fire and ash' was just about showcasing spectacular visuals and battle type moments.. but where sense of narrative clarity and story? in best trailers this decade you get strong sense of that so im just bit worried Fire and Ash may not be as great even if it breaks all time box office record- or as memorable as the orignal AVATAR... which is very very hard to top what youi all think?  And like Way of Water there is a chance if what i say turns out to be the case more broadly in hollywood that Fire and Ash would only be nominated in visual tech categories what you reckon?

So maybe Spider wasn't just the first human baby born on Pandora. He could have been a genetic experiment created by the RDA. It doesn't make sense that he could spontaneously develop Na’vi anatomy on his own or even through Eywa. Maybe Eywa saved him from imminent death (scene with the tendrils going into his mouth and nose), but I don't think they could've completely changed his physiology.

What I think is the RDA wanted to engineer a new kind of human who could survive on Pandora without a mask or an avatar body. As Jake warned, “If every human being on Earth could live here without a mask, then the Na’vi people will be gone.” 

Instead of only blending human DNA into Na’vi bodies to create avatars, the RDA may have tried the reverse: splicing Na’vi DNA into a human embryo, thus creating Spider. 

Either way, I'm pretty excited to see how it plays out.

1, It looks like the Ash People formed on their own, before the humans ever showed up. If so, good. I was always worried that Cameron’s saga would be about how ‘all evil comes from the humans.’ I like the idea that the Ash People’s anger is directed solely at ‘mother nature’ for betraying them.

2, Humans breathing on Pandora is a genuinely interesting new development - although (a) it kind of retcons some of the fundamental stakes of the world, and (b), how the hell would that even work logistically? If it was just him because of some biological anomaly, that’d be one thing, but they float the idea of all humans being able to breathe. So what could possibly have changed to allow this? Are they going to be adding magic to this franchise?

3, Quaritch teaming up with the Fire Queen is LAME. The most interesting thing about the Fire Na’vi was that they were a new faction with their own goals. They’re at war with the other Na’vi, but they wouldn’t necessarily like the Sky People either. So you’d have three factions fighting. That, to me, would have been way more interesting than just having the different bad guys team up for no reason. Why not have them trying to use each other. Maybe the Fire Queen attacks some stuff and blames the Sky People or something.

Also, Fire Queen wants to burn the world (to the point where she’s willing to ally with humans) just because…she likes fire and considers it pure? That’s so boring. Why not reveal that the Eywa religion has like a Vatican somewhere on Pandora and is a complex religion with a lot of corruption.

Or, maybe she’s originally from the Air Tribe and they have a strict doctrine of selflessness, and her childhood is ful of isolation and resentment because of how strict her parents were.
So she became the opposite - a power hungry maniac, and she was put on trial and imprisoned.
But the volcano destroyed her village - but spared her.
So she worships fire because it set her free; she feels she can use it to get anything she wants.

Am I the only one that isn't hyped. I love Avatar 1 its one of my favorite movies. But on the other hand I hate Avatar 2.
Avatar 1 is a movie with a heroes journey. Clear focus on the Main character. A film that stands on its own with a clear end and beginning. All of Pandora was new. Badass antagonist and epic end fight. Then part two lost focus of the Main character. Too many sidestories. They changed the Main reason why human wanted to go to Pandora. In part 1 it was a rare mineral. In part 2 out of nowhere some sort of life Elixier from a whale. No mention of the mineral at all for no reason. Suddenly you had no clear line between human and Navi like in part 1. All were blue exept some were evil. In part 1 I loved the changing between Avatar body and his human self. All gone. In part 1 All characters were interesting in part 2 I cant even remember most of them. And didnt notice how the bad Avatars died in the endfight that had stuff from Titanic and alien. Because part 2 was a half of the whole there were so many storylines that dont work without part 3. For example we got introduced to the female human antagonist but after appearing in the first 20 minutes she vanished nearly completely. Thats me but also I dont like them bringing back the antagonist of part 1. He was still good but not on the same level as it was in part 1. All he cared was revenge. Instead why not bring jack sullys dead Brother back. The one who died before Avatar 1 and why He was recruted at start. That could be interesting with him and his brother have different opinions and experiences. There was a reason why his dead brother signed the Deal.
Avatar 1 gave us jake sully at start without legs and in the end He was Running. First against navi at the end fighting with navi. First He was one without knowledge in the end He was the leader. Avatar 2 only gives us sully Running away at start and running away at the end. No progression made. 
For Avatar 3 I feel no need to introduce fire navi. Focus on the fight between human and navi that would be enough. The movie has no real focus in my opinion. I waited 12 years for Avatar 2 and I was deeply disappointed. Now I feel no hype at all. Maybe with that I can enjoy it a bit.

"Spare us your pity, alien. You gush about your connection with nature, your primal wisdom, but what has it brought you? Where are your marvels of engineering? Your voyages of discovery? Your great insight into the nature of the universe? Even at our basest, when we dressed as you do, dwelt as you do, hunted as you do, lived as you do, we did more than merely survive. We built wonders. We made great journeys. We forged epics. You have not. You speak so proudly of the plugs that dangle from your skulls, little realizing that they are but strings and you puppets. What little you have accomplished you attribute to the wisdom of your goddess, who is nothing but the voices of your dead echoing for all eternity. She moors you to the past, serving as a leash that keeps you as little better than apes, sad parodies of civilization that lack that special spark to become something more. We have come to your world in search of resources. Whether your actions drive us back or we take what we want and move on, the outcome is the same. We will depart from your wretched planet, leaving you behind. And in a thousand years, you will not have changed from this contact with another world. You will remain in your trees, hunting your prey, communing with your goddess, until your sun burns out and your world dies. And above your tomb, the stars will belong to us."

I know this is a bit nitpicky, but one thing I don't get about this series is the idea that Humanity can travel between star systems and build an entire city on an alien world in mere days, but the Earth is somehow too far gone to ever be saved. The corporate stuff I get with rich A-holes wanting to profit off of the new resources that Pandora has and Earth doesn't. The whole colonization thing I don't get. The Earth has bounced back from worse than what we are doing to it. Why don't these future Humans just use their super advanced space and construction tech to build colonies elsewhere in Earth's Solar System and move as much of the environment destroying industry off of Earth as possible and start harvesting resources from Mars or the moons of the gas giants or the Asteroid Belt? Why is colonizing a world whose astosphere is toxic to us without some form of Pandoran body modification like Spider apparently has happen to him a more viable option than spreading throughout our own Solar System? They clearly have the technology to do it.

I honestly think we’re entering a new Golden Age of cinema, storytelling, and directing. Anime creators are raising the bar with fresh series and movies, video games like Monster Hunter are pushing creativity, and even some gacha games are stepping up. Now we’ve got the Demon Slayer: Infinity Castle movie, plus James Cameron never missing with the Avatar films. On top of that, AI and top-tier CGI designers are fine-tuning visuals like never before, since the technology is finally up to par compared to years ago. Elite cinema is BACK.

James Cameron isn't afraid to tell the whole plot of this movie through the trailer. He simply cracked the code. Providing audience the movie going experience is his whole thing now.

The visuals continue to be stunning, but I'm hoping this installment breaks new narrative ground. The first film was groundbreaking, but the sequel felt like familiar territory - gorgeous to look at, yet retreading themes we've seen before. 

What I'd love to see is the franchise move beyond surface-level appropriation of indigenous wisdom toward genuinely innovative storytelling that challenges Western individualism and binary thinking. The concept of interconnectedness has so much untapped potential - imagine exploring truly collective consciousness, non-hierarchical societies, or posthuman ways of being that transcend the human/nature divide.

The military-industrial framing also feels limiting when the source material offers opportunities for more complex conflict resolution. I'm still excited to see it and genuinely hope Cameron surprises us with deeper philosophical territory this time around (probably not).

Oh yeah, thats right. They have a franchise. Sorry Jim, lost interest and moved on. You know, these movies and TV shows with their YEARS going by before they do a sequel or next season (especially TV, my goodness. I want the 26 episode format back, which starts in the fall and end around May, and then the next season is in the fall again). There are exceptions. Like Aliens, which was 7 years later (And Cameron ironically). But this is one of those specific ones that lost momentum, and I lost interest in. Same with Stranger Things. Sorry, you lost the momentum, and years go by and you can't expect me to just jump right in. Other shows come along, and are hot, and I'm not interested in Stranger things anymore. Anyway, with Avatar, honestly, letting it marinate after all these years, I didn't think it was this amazing thing, and didn't warrant sequels. It was about the effects. But take that away, and what are you left with? I'm not watching a movie for cool effects. Meh. So yeah, I've checked out, and not interested.
